questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of material culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The smartphones, cars, and clothing produced and used within a society.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A large coordinated campaign pressing lawmakers for new environmental protections.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A nation celebrating many groups' festivals and languages rather than promoting a single culture.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Laws and etiquette failing to keep pace with a newly invented communication technology.

questions.explanation: An example of material culture is: the smartphones, cars, and clothing produced and used within a society. In general, material culture refers to the physical objects and artifacts that members of a society create and use. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Before picking an example, recall the concept: what is material culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The physical objects and artifacts a society creates and uses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The shared beliefs, values, and norms of a society.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tendency to judge other cultures by one's own standards.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Organized efforts by many people to promote social change.

hints.rationale: Material culture is the tangible objects a society makes and uses, unlike its nonmaterial ideas.

hints.prompt: Which feature must an example show to count as material culture rather than nonmaterial culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It must compare two different societies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It must involve a large group acting together.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It must state a rule that people are expected to follow.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It must be a tangible, physical object.

hints.rationale: Physical tangibility is what separates material culture from nonmaterial values and norms.

hints.prompt: One tempting choice describes laws and etiquette failing to keep pace with a new communication technology. Why is it NOT the best example of material culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It defines shared beliefs, which is what material culture means.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is actually the clearest example of material culture.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It never mentions technology, so it is irrelevant.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It focuses on norms lagging behind, not on the physical object itself.

hints.rationale: That option centers on nonmaterial norms lagging behind an invention, not the artifact itself.

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: the tendency to evaluate other cultures by the standards of one's own culture, seen as superior?

choices.label: A
choices.text: globalization

choices.label: B
choices.text: acculturation

choices.label: C
choices.text: ethnocentrism

choices.label: D
choices.text: stigma

questions.explanation: This description matches ethnocentrism, which is the tendency to evaluate other cultures by the standards of one's own culture, seen as superior. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.

hints.prompt: This item describes a concept without naming it. Which statement best captures that idea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The worldwide integration of markets, ideas, and goods.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A mark or attribute that deeply discredits a person.

choices.label: C

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has nothing to do with culture.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It fits because it also implies cultural superiority.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is just another name for the described concept.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes assimilation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The view that deviance arises when people cannot reach valued goals through legitimate means.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The process by which a minority group gradually takes on the culture of the dominant group.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The process by which societies move toward industrial, urban, and rationalized forms.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Widespread cultural patterns that are accessible to most members of a society.

questions.explanation: Assimilation is best described as the process by which a minority group gradually takes on the culture of the dominant group. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Assimilation is best understood as which kind of process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A model of how economies industrialize.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A type of mass entertainment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A process of cultural change between groups.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A theory about why people commit deviance.

hints.rationale: Assimilation is fundamentally cultural change between a minority and a dominant group.

hints.prompt: Which detail defines assimilation specifically, as opposed to other kinds of cultural change?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A group deliberately rejects the dominant culture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two cultures merge into a brand-new third culture.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A minority group takes on the culture of the dominant group.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Traits spread evenly between two equal societies.

hints.rationale: Assimilation specifically means a minority adopting the dominant group's culture, not equal exchange or rejection.

hints.prompt: One choice describes cultural patterns that are widely accessible to most members of a society. Why is it NOT assimilation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It never mentions culture, so it cannot be assimilation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It describes economic development instead of culture.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is actually a better definition of assimilation than the others.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It describes broadly shared popular culture, not a minority adopting the dominant culture.

hints.rationale: That option is about widely shared cultural patterns, not the one-way cultural absorption assimilation describes.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of mores?

choices.text: A movement gaining momentum only after securing funding, volunteers, and press coverage.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intense public outrage directed at someone who defrauds a vulnerable elderly person.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A student cheating once without ever coming to see themselves as a cheater.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A graduation ceremony with caps, gowns, and the turning of tassels.

questions.explanation: An example of mores is: intense public outrage directed at someone who defrauds a vulnerable elderly person. In general, mores refers to strongly held norms with moral significance whose violation brings serious condemnation. The other options illustrate different concepts.

hints.prompt: Before choosing an example, recall the concept: what are mores?
